input_metadata.original_text: සිද්ධි වුණේ 2024 ජනවාරි 12 වෙනිදා රැ 11:45 ට විතර. මමයි මගේ සැමියා වන සිරිසේනයි අපේ පුතා කමලුයි ගෙදර නිදාගෙන හිටියේ. එකපාරටම අපේ ගෙදර ඉස්සරහා දොර කඩාගෙන පස් දෙනෙක් ගේ ඇතුලට කඩා වැදුනා. ඒ අයගෙන් තුන් දෙනෙකු මම අදුර ගත්තා. ප්‍රධාන සැකකරු වන චන්ඩි මල්ලි කියන කෙනා අත්තේ T56 වර්ගයේ ගිනි අවියක් තිබුණා. අනිත් අයත් ඒ යකඩ පොලු සහ මන්න පිහි තිබුණා. චන්ඩි මල්ලි කෑ ගහලා කිව්වා අද තොපි ඉතුරු කරන්නේ නැහැ. ඉඩම් නඩුව ඉල්ලා අස් කර ගත්තේ නැත්නම් මරණවා කියලා. එහෙම කියලා එයා මගේ සැමියාගේ පපුවට වෙඩි තිබ්බා. සැමියා බිම වැටුණා. එතකොටම අනිත් අය මගේ පුතා කමල්ට යකඩ පොලු වලින් ගැහුවා. කමල්ගේ ඔලුව පැලිලා ලේ ගොඩක් ගියා. මම කෑ ගහනකොට ඒ අය මගේ බෙල්ලට පිහිය තියලා කිව්වා මේක කාට හරි කිව්වොත් තෝවත් මරණවා කියලා. ඊට පස්සේ ඒ අය ගෙදර තිබුණ රත්තරන් බඩුයි රුපියල් ලක්ෂ දෙකක සල්ලි අරගෙන පැනලා ගියා. යනකොට චන්ඩි මල්ලි කිව්වා අපි යන්නේ පොලොන්නරුවට. පොලීසියට කිව්වොත් බලාගමු කියලා. අපි සැමියාවයි පුතාවයි රෝහලට ගෙනිච්චා. සැමියා රෝහලට ගෙනියන අතරතුර මට කිව්වා මට වෙඩි තිබ්බේ චන්ඩි මල්ලි තමයි කියලා. දොස්තර මහත්තර කිව්වා සැමියා මැරිලා කියලා. පුතා තාම දැඩි සත්කාර ඒකකයේ ඉන්නේ. හැබැයි දැන් චන්ඩි මල්ලිගේ නීතිඥයෝ කියනවා සිද්ධිය වෙන වෙලාවේ චන්ඩි මල්ලි හිටියේ කිලෝමීටර් 100ක් එහයින් තියෙන මංගල උත්සවයක කියලා. ඒකට සාක්ෂි විදිහට වීඩියෝ පටයක් ඉදිරිපත් කරලා තියෙනවා. ඒ වගේම පොලීසිය චන්ඩි මල්ලීව අත්අඩංගුවට ගත්තාම එයා පෙන්නපු තැනක තිබිලා වෙඩි තියපු තුවක්කුව හොයා ගත්තා.
input_metadata.analyzed_text: The incident occurred on January 12, 2024, around 11:45 PM. My husband, Sirisena, our son, Kamalu, and I were sleeping at home. Suddenly, five individuals broke through our front door and entered the house. I recognized three of them. The main suspect, named Chandi Malli, had a T56-type firearm in his hand. The others had iron rods and machetes. Chandi Malli shouted and stated, "Today, we will not spare you. If you do not withdraw the land case, we will kill you." Having said that, he shot my husband in the chest. My husband fell to the ground. At that moment, the others struck my son Kamal with iron rods. Kamal's head was split open, and he bled profusely. When I screamed, they placed a knife to my neck and warned, "If you tell anyone about this, we will kill you too." After that, they took the gold jewelry and two hundred thousand rupees from the house and fled. As they were leaving, Chandi Malli stated, "We are going to Polonnaruwa. If you inform the police, we will see." We took my husband and son to the hospital. While my husband was being transported to the hospital, he told me, "It was Chandi Malli who shot me." The doctor informed us that my husband had died. My son is still in the Intensive Care Unit. However, Chandi Malli's lawyers are now asserting that at the time of the incident, Chandi Malli was at a wedding ceremony 100 kilometers away. A video clip has been presented as evidence for this. Furthermore, when the police arrested Chandi Malli, they recovered the firearm used in the shooting from a location he indicated.

legal_analysis.prosecution_argument:
The prosecution will argue that Chandi Malli and his accomplices committed several grave offenses, including murder, grievous hurt, robbery, and criminal intimidation. The incident clearly demonstrates a targeted home invasion with the express motive of intimidating the family to withdraw a land case, culminating in extreme violence and theft. 

Firstly, the elements of **Robbery (DOC_0)** are fully satisfied. The intruders broke into the home, caused the death of Sirisena, caused grievous hurt to Kamalu, and placed a knife to the narrator's neck, instilling fear of instant death or hurt. This violence was directly linked to the 'committing of the theft' of gold jewelry and two hundred thousand rupees. The initial threat, 'Today, we will not spare you. If you do not withdraw the land case, we will kill you,' establishes the intent and connection between the violence and the underlying dispute, and the subsequent theft. The theft itself is defined in **DOC_1** as dishonestly taking movable property without consent.

Secondly, the murder of Sirisena is evidenced by the direct shot to his chest by Chandi Malli. This is powerfully supported by the dying declaration of Sirisena to his wife, identifying Chandi Malli as the shooter. The admissibility of such statements of deceased persons, especially regarding the circumstances of their death, is crucial (referenced in **DOC_11** regarding Section 32(1) of the Evidence Ordinance, though not explicitly provided here).

Thirdly, the grievous hurt inflicted on Kamalu, whose head was split open with iron rods, constitutes attempted murder or grievous hurt. 

Finally, the most compelling piece of evidence against Chandi Malli is the recovery of the T56-type firearm used in the shooting from a location he himself indicated to the police. This is admissible under Section 27 of the Evidence Ordinance (as mentioned in **DOC_5**) and directly links him to the instrument of the crime, providing irrefutable evidence against his alibi. The wife's direct eyewitness identification further corroborates Chandi Malli's presence and actions.
legal_analysis.defense_argument:
The defense for Chandi Malli will focus on establishing his alibi and challenging the reliability of the prosecution's identification evidence. The primary argument is that Chandi Malli was not present at the scene of the crime, but was instead 100 kilometers away at a wedding ceremony, for which a video clip serves as concrete evidence.

Firstly, the defense will argue that the witness identification by the narrator is unreliable due to the circumstances of the incident: late night (11:45 PM), extreme duress, and the chaotic nature of the attack involving multiple assailants. The memory and perception under such stressful conditions can be significantly impaired. The defense may also question if the light conditions were sufficient for a clear identification.

Secondly, while a dying declaration is strong evidence, the defense may challenge its specific wording or context if there's any ambiguity, or argue that the victim, in his final moments of severe pain and shock, might have been mistaken or influenced. While **DOC_11** concerns letters from the deceased, its principles regarding expressions 'not directly related to the circumstances of the transaction' could be adapted to challenge the *specificity* or *unambiguity* of the dying declaration, if possible.

Thirdly, regarding the recovery of the firearm, the defense will scrutinize the police procedures detailed in **DOC_5** and **DOC_8**. The defense will question how Chandi Malli 'indicated' the location of the firearm. Was this indication voluntary, or was it coerced? Could Chandi Malli have known the location of the firearm without being the actual perpetrator, perhaps by being informed by another individual involved? The defense could argue that the police investigation was flawed or that the evidence chain for the firearm is compromised.

Finally, the defense may also invoke principles from **DOC_10 (K. G. Somapala)** to object to any attempt by the prosecution to introduce 'bad character' evidence related to the 'land case' motive, unless it is strictly necessary and directly relevant to proving the *actus reus* or *mens rea* of the immediate offense, arguing that such evidence is highly prejudicial.

matches.title: Robbery
matches.excerpt:
In all robbery there is theft or extortion

When a Theft is 'robbery', if, in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end, voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt or of instant wrongful restraint.

Extortion is 'robbery', if the offender, is at the time of committing the extortion, is in the presence of the person put in fear and commits the extortion by putting that person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint to that person or to some other person, and, by so putting in fear, induces the person so put in fear then and there to deliver up the thing extorted.

Explanation

The offender is said to be present if he is sufficiently near to put the other person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint.

Illustrations

(a) A holds Z down, and fraudulently takes Z's money and jewels from Z's clothes, without Z's consent. Here A has committed theft, and, in order to the committing of that theft, has voluntarily caused wrongful restraint to Z. A has therefore committed robbery.

(b) A meets Z on the high road, shows a pistol, and demands Zs purse. Z, in consequence surrenders his purse- Here A has extorted the purse from Z by putting him in fear of instant hurt, and being at the time of committing the extortion in his presence, A has therefore committed robbery.

(c) A meets Z and Zs child on the high road. A takes the child and threatens to fling it down a precipice unless Z delivers his purse. Z, in consequence, delivers his purse. Here A has extorted the purse from Z by causing Z to be in fear of instant hurt to the child who is there present. A has therefore committed robbery on Z.

(d) A obtains property from Z by saying: 'Your child is in the hands of my gang, and will be put to death unless you send us ten thousand rupees '. This is extortion, and punishable as such; but it is not robbery, unless Z is put in fear of the instant death of his child.

matches.title: Theft
matches.excerpt:
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Explanation 1

A thing so long as it is attached to the earth, not being movable property, is not the subject of theft; but it becomes capable of being the subject of theft as soon as it is severed from the earth.

Explanation 2

A moving effected by the same act which effects the severance may be a theft.

Explanation 3

A person is said to cause a thing to move by removing an obstacle which prevented it from moving, or by separating it from any other thing, as well as by actually moving it.

Explanation 4

A person who by any means causes an animal to move is said to move that animal and to move everything which in consequence of the motion so caused, is moved by that animal.

Explanation 5

The consent mentioned in the definition may be expressed or implied, and may be given either by the person in possession, or by any person having for that purpose authority either express or implied.

Illustrations

(a) A cuts down a tree on Z's ground, with the intention of dishonestly taking the tree out of Z's possession without Z's consent. Here, as soon as A has severed the tree, in order to such taking, he has committed theft.

(b) A puts a bait for dogs in his pocket, and thus induces Z's dog to follow it. Here, if As intention be dishonestly to take the dog out of Z's possession without Z's consent, A has committed theft as soon as Z's dog has begun to follow A.

(c) A meets a bullock carrying a box of treasure. He drives the bullock in a certain direction, in order that he may dishonestly take the treasure. As soon as the bullock begins to move A has committed theft of the treasure.

(d) A, being Z's servant, and entrusted by Z with the care of Z's plate, dishonestly runs away with the plate without Z's consent. A has committed theft.

(e) Z, going on a journey, entrusts his plate to A, the keeper of a warehouse, till Z shall return. A carries the plate to a goldsmith and sells it. Here the plate was not in Z's possession. It could not therefore be taken out of Z's possession, and A has not committed theft, though he may have committed criminal breach of trust.

(f) A finds a ring belonging to Z on a table in the house which Z occupies. Here the ring is in Z's possession, and if A dishonestly removes it, A commits theft.

(g) A finds a ring lying on the high road, not in the possession of any person. A, by taking it, commits no theft, though he may commit criminal misappropriation of property,

(h) A sees a ring belonging to Z lying on a table in Z's house. Not venturing to misappropriate the ring immediately for fear of search and detection, A hides the ring in a place where it is highly improbable that it will ever be found by Z, with the intention of taking the ring from the hiding place and selling it when the loss is forgotten- Here A. at the time of first moving the ring, commits theft.

(i) A delivers his watch to Z, a jeweler, to be regulated, Z carries it to his shop. A, not owing to the jeweler any debt for which the jeweler might lawfully detain the watch as security, enters the shop openly, takes his watch by force out of Z's hand, and carries it away. Here A, though he may have committed criminal trespass and assault, has not committed theft, inasmuch as what he did was not done dishonestly.

(j) If A owes money to Z for repairing the watch, and if Z retains the watch lawfully as a security for the debt, and A takes the watch out of Zs possession with the intention of depriving Z of the property as a security for his debt, he commits theft, inasmuch as he takes it dishonestly.

(k) Again, if A having pawned his watch to Z, takes it out of Z's possession without Z's consent, not having paid what he borrowed on the watch, he commits theft, though the watch is his own property, inasmuch as he takes it dishonestly.

(l) A takes an article belonging to Z out of Z's possession, without Z's consent, with the intention of keeping it until he obtains money from Z as a reward for its restoration. Here A takes dishonestly; A has therefore committed theft.

(m) A, being on friendly terms with Z, goes in to Z's library in Z's absence, and takes away a book without Z's express consent, for the purpose merely of reading it, and with the intention of returning it. Here, it is probable that A may have conceived that he had Z's implied consent to use Zs book. If this was A's impression, A has not committed theft.

(n) A asks charity from Z's wife. She gives A money, food, and clothes, which A knows to belong to Z, her husband. Here, it is probable that A may conceive that Z's wife is authorized to give away alms. If this was A's impression, A has not committed theft.

(o) A is the paramour of Z's wife. She gives A valuable property which A knows to belong to her husband Z and to be such property as she has not authority from Z to give. If A takes the property dishonestly, he commits theft.

(p) A in good faith, believing property belonging to Z to be A's own property, takes that property out of B's possession. Here, as A does not take dishonestly, he does not commit theft.

matches.title: Dishonest misappropriation of property
matches.excerpt:
Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both

Illustration

(a) A takes property belonging to Z out of Z*'s possession, in good faith believing, at the time when he takes it, that the properly belongs to himself. A is not guilty of theft; but if A, after discovering his mistake, dishonestly appropriates the property to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

(b) A, being on friendly terms with Z, goes into Z's library in Z's absence, and takes away a book without Z's express consent. Here, if A was under the impression that he had Z's implied consent to take the book for the purpose of reading it. A has not committed theft. But if A afterwards sells the book for his own benefit, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

(c) A and B being joint owners of a horse, A takes the horse out of B's possession, intending to use it. Here as A has a right to use the horse, he does not dishonestly misappropriate it. But if A sells the horse and appropriates the whole proceeds to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

Explanation I

A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is misappropriation within the meaning of this section.

Illustration

A finds a promissory note belonging to Z, payable to bearer. A, knowing that the note belongs to Z, pledges it with a banker as security for a loan, intending at a future time to restore it to Z. A has committed an offence under this section.

Explanation 2

(i) A person who finds property not in the possession of any other person and takes such property for the purpose of protecting it for, or of restoring it to, the owner, does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly, and is not guilty of an offence; but he is guilty of the offence above defined if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner, or before he has used reasonable means to discover and give notice to the owner, and has kept the properly a reasonable time to enable the owner to claim it.
(ii) What are reasonable means, or what is a reasonable time in such a case, is a question of fact.
(iii) It is not necessary that the finder should know who is the owner of the property, or that any particular person is the owner of it; it is sufficient if, at the time of appropriating it, he does not believe it to be his own property, or in good faith believes that the real owner cannot be found.

Illustration

(a) A finds a rupee on the high road, not knowing to whom the rupee belongs. A picks up the rupee. Here A has not committed the offence defined in this section.
(b) A finds a letter on the road containing a bank note. From the direction and contents of the letter he learns to whom the note belongs. He appropriates the note. He is guilty of an offence under this section.
(c) A finds a cheque payable to bearer. He can form no conjecture as to the person who has lost the cheque. But the name of the person who has drawn the cheque appears. A knows that this person can direct him to the person in whose favour the cheque was drawn. A appropriates the cheque without attempting to discover the owner. He is guilty of an offence under this section.
(d) A sees Z drop no purse with money in it. A picks up the purse with the intention of restoring it to Z, but afterwards appropriates it to his own use. A has committed an offence under this section.
(e) A finds a purse with money not knowing to whom it belongs; he afterwards discovers that it belongs to Z, and appropriates it to his own use. A is guilty of an offence under this section.
(f) A finds a valuable ring, not knowing to whom it belongs. A sells it immediately, without attempting to discover the owner. A is guilty of an offence under this section.

matches.title: Criminal breach of trust
matches.excerpt:
Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or willfully suffers any other person so to do, commits 'criminal breach of trust'.

Illustrations

(a) A, being executor to the will of a deceased person, dishonestly disobeys the law, which directs him to divide the effects according to the will, and appropriates them to his own use. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(b) A is a warehouse-keeper. Z, going on a journey. entrusts his furniture to A, under a contract that it shall be returned on payment of a stipulated sum for warehouse room. A dishonestly sells the goods. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(c) A, residing in Colombo, is agent for Z, residing in England, There is an express or implied contract between A and Z that all sums remitted by Z to A shall be Invested by A according to Z's direction, Z remits ten thousand rupees to A, with directions to A to invest the same on mortgage of coffee estates, A dishonestly disobeys the directions, and employs the money in his own business. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(d) But if A, in the last illustration, not dishonestly but in good faith, believing that it will be more for Z's advantage to hold shares in a company, disobeys Z's directions and buys shares in a company in Z's name instead of investing the money on mortgage, here, though Z should suffer loss, and should be entitled to bring a civil action against A on account of that loss, yet A not having acted dishonestly has not committed criminal breach of trust.

(e) A, a revenue officer, is entrusted with public money, and is either directed by law or bound by a contract, express or implied, with the Government, to pay into a certain Kachcheri all the public money which he holds. A dishonestly appropriates the money. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(f) A, a carrier, is entrusted by Z with properly to be carried by land or by water. A dishonestly misappropriates the property. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

matches.title: Extortion
matches.excerpt:
Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person or to any other and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits ' extortion '.

Illustrations

(a) A threatens to publish a defamatory libel concerning Z, unless Z gives him money. He thus induces Z to give him money. A has committed extortion.

(b) A threatens Z that he will keep Z's child in wrongful confinement, unless Z will sign and deliver to A promissory note binding Z to pay certain moneys to A, Z signs and delivers the note. A has committed extortion.

(c) A, by putting Z in fear of grievous hurt, dishonestly induces Z to sign or affix his seal to a blank paper and deliver it to A. Z signs and delivers the paper to A. Here, as the paper so signed may be converted into a valuable security, A has committed extortion.

matches.title: Examination of witnesses by police officer or inquirer.
matches.excerpt: (1) Details the procedure for recording statements from witnesses during investigation: no oath, full recording in a specific manner, reading back/interpretation, liberty to explain/add, signing, and a certificate by the officer. (2) The person is bound to answer all questions except those tending to incriminate themselves. (3) Specifies the limited use of statements made to police (not for corroboration, but for contradiction or under S.27 of Evidence Ordinance). (4) Courts may send for statements recorded in an investigation and may use such statements or information, not as evidence in the case, but to aid it in such inquiry or trial. Save as otherwise provided for in section 444 neither the accused nor his agents shall be entitled to call for such statements, nor shall he or they be entitled to see them merely because they are referred to by the court but if they are used by the police officer or inquirer or witness who made them to refresh his memory, or if the court uses them for the purpose of contradicting such police officer or inquirer or witness the provisions of the Evidence Ordinance, section 161 or section 145, as the case may be, shall apply: Provided that where a preliminary inquiry under Chapter XV is being held in respect of any offence, such statements of witnesses as have up to then been recorded shall, on the application of the accused, be made available to him for his perusal in open court during the inquiry.

matches.title: Plea of guilty and sentencing with written plea agreement.
matches.excerpt: (1) A plea agreement may be entered into between the prosecutor and accused before sentence. (2) The court shall be informed but not participate. (3) The prosecutor, accused's attorney, or accused may initiate, but the prosecutor has sole discretion. (4) For certain offences, the accused must be fingerprinted. (5)-(7) Details the negotiation process, the prosecutor's duties (considering circumstances, not using info if negotiations fail, allowing victim input). (8) Details on sentence recommendations, court's discretion, and consequences of parties deviating from the agreement. (9)-(10) Procedure for presenting the plea agreement and court acceptance. (11) Upon acceptance, the agreement is binding, and the accused is convicted. (12)-(13) Limits on appeals against conviction and sentence. (14)-(16) Procedure and consequences if the court rejects the agreement. (17) Defines 'child' and 'prosecutor'.

matches.title: Medical examination in case of an offence alleged to have been committed by a child of, or above, twelve years of age and under fourteen.
matches.excerpt: (1) The OIC investigating an offence by a child (12-14 years) shall, with parental consent, have the child examined by a multidisciplinary team to assess maturity and responsibility. (2) The team comprises a judicial medical officer, a pediatric/adolescent psychiatrist, and a psychologist. (3) If consent is refused, the OIC shall apply to the Magistrate for an order. (4)-(6) Details procedures for obtaining the report, subjecting the child to rehabilitation, and submitting the report to the Magistrate.

matches.title: Taking of finger impressions, specimens of hair & c. of suspect person.
matches.excerpt: (1) An OIC may, with consent, take bodily impressions, specimens (blood, saliva, etc.), or scrapings from a suspect. (2) If consent is refused, the OIC may apply to the Magistrate for an order. (3) An OIC may, with consent, take a specimen of handwriting. (4) If refusal, the OIC may apply to the Magistrate for an order.

matches.title: Thilakaratne v. Attorney-General
matches.excerpt: [{'point_number': 1, 'summary': 'Where an accused is being tried in absentia under section 241(1) of the Criminal Procedure Code, it is undesirable for the court to assign a counsel to defend him without his consent. Although intended for his benefit, such a step would trigger section 241(4), thereby depriving the accused of the valuable right to re-open the proceedings under section 241(3) if he later proves his absence was bona fide.'}, {'point_number': 2, 'summary': 'Section 195(g) of the Criminal Procedure Code, which provides for assigning counsel, contemplates a situation where the accused is physically present in court and requests counsel upon being questioned.'}, {'point_number': 3, 'summary': 'Section 241(2) does not make it obligatory for the court to assign counsel to an absent accused. This section applies where an absent accused has retained counsel or where a counsel continues to appear for an accused who absconds during the trial.'}] The 1st, 2nd, and 3rd accused were charged in the High Court of Anuradhapura on two counts: (1) Robbery of cash Rs. 18,552/40 and a bicycle, causing hurt to Ukkubanda Semasinghe, punishable under sections 380 and 382 read with section 32 of the Penal Code; and (2) Robbery of a bicycle from Punchi Bandage Ratnayake, punishable under section 380 read with section 32. The accused appeared on 22.9.1986 represented by counsel. On the trial date 17.1.86, the 1st accused was absent without excuse. A warrant was issued for 20.1.87. On 20.1.87, the 1st accused was initially present in the court premises but absconded before the case was called. His surety testified that the accused had come to court but left. The trial Judge, satisfied that the 1st accused was absconding, proceeded with the trial in absentia. The 2nd and 3rd accused pleaded guilty and were sentenced. The trial proceeded against the 1st accused. Evidence was led showing the accused had robbed a school principal and teacher of payroll money, armed with a pistol and knife. The 1st accused was found guilty and sentenced to 10 years rigorous imprisonment and fines. The 1st accused was arrested and produced in court on 7.12.87. On 13.1.88, his counsel submitted he had been unwell but tendered no medical certificate. The High Court Judge refused bail and ordered the sentence to be carried out. No application was made under section 241(3) of the Criminal Procedure Code to show bona fide absence and reopen the case. An appeal was filed arguing insufficient evidence of absconding and failure to assign counsel. The Court rejected the ground that there was insufficient evidence of absconding. The accused was present on the morning of the trial but left without excuse. It was only a year later that a claim of illness was made, unsupported by any medical certificate. The conclusion that the 1st accused was absconding was inescapable. The Court addressed the argument that the failure to assign counsel vitiated the conviction. Section 195(g) applies only when an accused is present. While Section 241(2) ensures an absent accused's right to be defended is not prejudiced, it does not mandate the court to assign counsel. Crucially, Section 241(4) prevents an accused defended by an attorney from reopening proceedings under Section 241(3). Therefore, assigning counsel to an absent accused without consent is detrimental as it deprives them of the statutory right to reopen the case if they can prove bona fide absence. The trial Judge's conduct in not assigning counsel was correct. The Court considered the disparity in sentences between the 1st accused (10 years) and the others (3 years). The disparity was justified because the 1st accused was armed with a pistol, fired a shot, caused extensive injuries with a knife, and had previous convictions. The appeal was dismissed, and the conviction and sentences were affirmed.

matches.title: K. G. Somapala v. The Attorney-General
matches.excerpt: [{'point_number': 1, 'summary': 'In a criminal prosecution, evidence of the bad character of the accused, which is not necessary or relevant to prove the alleged motive for the offence, is inadmissible.'}, {'point_number': 2, 'summary': "Where the prosecution led evidence that the accused's work was stopped because he had attempted to stab an Engineer, purportedly to prove motive for assaulting a complainant suspected of instigating the stoppage, such evidence was held to be inadmissible as it related to bad character and was not necessary to prove the motive."}] The Accused-Appellant was charged for voluntarily causing hurt to one B. Robert Silva, a driver in the Ceylon Transport Board with hands, punishable under Section 314 of the Ceylon Penal Code. Accused was a mechanic employed in the Ceylon Transport Board. He was found guilty of the charge and was sentenced to 2 months' rigorous imprisonment and to pay a fine of Rs. 50/-. In default of payment of the fine, he was sentenced to one month's rigorous imprisonment. At the trial, the prosecution led evidence of motive for this offence. Robert Silva stated that the accused was under the impression that he had instigated the Engineer to stop the accused's work. He further stated that accused's work was stopped as a result of his attempt to stab the Engineer of the Ceylon Transport Board. Clearly the evidence that accused's work was stopped because he attempted to stab the Engineer of the Ceylon Transport Board was not necessary or relevant for the purpose of proving the motive that the accused had against the complainant Robert Silva. The alleged motive that Accused had against the Complainant was that he suspected that the Complainant was instrumental in having him interdicted from work over another incident. Learned Crown Counsel conceded that this evidence was inadmissible to prove the alleged motive. He argued, however, that no prejudice has been caused to the accused as the learned Magistrate has not referred to this aspect of the evidence in his reasons for the conviction. It is quite clear in this case that the Magistrate has taken a very serious view of the offence of this accused in view of the sentence passed in this case. There is no doubt that he was strongly influenced by the evidence that the accused had previously attempted to stab the C. T. B. Engineer. He commenced his reasons by stating, 'The sooner the Ceylon Transport Board gets rid of undesirable people like the accused the better it is for this establishment'. Even if it can be said in this case which I am not prepared to do, that the learned Magistrate was in fact not prejudiced against the accused by this inadmissible evidence of accused's previous bad character, this evidence is very likely to prejudice any judge deciding this case. In V. Coomaraswamy v. M. Meera Saibo and 5 others it was held 'It is not suggested that the learned Magistrate was influenced by these statements but the accused may very well have gained the impression that he was so influenced. It is necessary not only that administration of justice should be pure but it should be seen to be pure and considered to be pure'. In this case I have carefully considered whether this case should be sent back for a retrial as the conviction of the accused cannot possibly stand. Considering the trivial nature of the charge and the nature of the evidence in support of the prosecution case, I do not think that the accused should be put to the trouble and expense of standing another trial in this case. I accordingly allow the appeal and set aside the conviction and sentence of the accused and acquit him.

matches.title: The Queen v. M. Sathasivam
matches.excerpt: [{'point_number': 1, 'summary': "A letter written by a deceased person to the police expressing fear or suspicion of the accused, in anticipation of the accused's return from abroad, is not admissible under Section 32(1) of the Evidence Ordinance if it amounts to mere general expressions not directly related to the circumstances of the transaction resulting in death."}, {'point_number': 2, 'summary': 'Such a letter is not admissible to prove motive under Section 8(1) of the Evidence Ordinance unless there is independent evidence that the allegations in the letter had come to the knowledge of the accused and induced resentment in his mind.'}, {'point_number': 3, 'summary': 'The letter is not admissible to prove conduct of the deceased under Section 8(2) read with Section 11 unless there is independent evidence to support the suggestion that the deceased was apprehensive of danger to her safety.'}, {'point_number': 4, 'summary': 'Evidence of trifling weight which is gravely prejudicial to the accused, even if technically admissible, ought to be excluded by the Judge if its potential prejudice is out of proportion to its true evidential value.'}] The prisoner is charged with murdering his wife on the morning of 9th October, 1951. The prisoner had been in England while the deceased remained in Ceylon. Divorce proceedings for malicious desertion had been instituted by the deceased. The Crown sought to introduce a letter (P24) written by the deceased on 17th September 1951 to the Superintendent of Police, Colombo, anticipating the prisoner's return and expressing fear that he might use violence and requesting police protection. The defence objected to the reception of the letter P24. The Solicitor-General argued it was admissible under Section 32(1) of the Evidence Ordinance. The Court held that the statements in the letter amounted to mere general expressions indicating fear or suspicion and were not directly related to the occasion of her death. Citing Narayana Swami v. Emperor, the Court ruled that the circumstances must have some proximate relation to the actual occurrence. Thus, the letter was not admissible under Section 32(1). The Crown argued the letter was relevant under Section 8(1) to establish motive. The theory was that the prisoner's knowledge of the letter caused resentment. The Court held that for this to be admissible, the Crown must prove the prisoner actually knew of the letter and resented it. Without independent evidence of such knowledge and resentment, the letter could not be admitted to prove motive. The Court distinguished R. v. Buckley, where prior threats were admitted because there was a direct link to the motive. The Crown alternatively argued admissibility under Section 8(2) as evidence of the deceased's conduct (seeking police protection due to fear). The Court found this relevance remote. The suggestion that her cordiality towards the accused was feigned due to fear required independent evidence of her apprehension. Until such evidence was forthcoming, Section 8(2) did not apply. The Court distinguished the Indian case Golak Behari Takal v. The Emperor, noting that in that case, the complaint related to the specific crime (conspiracy) under investigation. The Court emphasized the principle from Noor Mohamed v. The King and Harris v. D.P.P. that a Judge should exclude evidence that has only trifling weight but is gravely prejudicial to the accused. The evidential value of P24 standing alone was slender, while its prejudicial effect on the jury would be substantial. Justice required that unless the truth of the document's contents could be proved by independent evidence for a relevant purpose, it should be excluded to ensure a fair trial. The objection to the reception of the letter P24 and related oral evidence was upheld. The document was ruled out for the present, subject to any fresh application by the Crown if independent evidence establishing its relevance became available later.

data.label: Robbery
data.excerpt:
In all robbery there is theft or extortion

When a Theft is 'robbery', if, in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end, voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt or of instant wrongful restraint.

Extortion is 'robbery', if the offender, is at the time of committing the extortion, is in the presence of the person put in fear and commits the extortion by putting that person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint to that person or to some other person, and, by so putting in fear, induces the person so put in fear then and there to deliver up the thing extorted.

Explanation

The offender is said to be present if he is sufficiently near to put the other person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint.

Illustrations

(a) A holds Z down, and fraudulently takes Z's money and jewels from Z's clothes, without Z's consent. Here A has committed theft, and, in order to the committing of that theft, has voluntarily caused wrongful restraint to Z. A has therefore committed robbery.

(b) A meets Z on the high road, shows a pistol, and demands Zs purse. Z, in consequence surrenders his purse- Here A has extorted the purse from Z by putting him in fear of instant hurt, and being at the time of committing the extortion in his presence, A has therefore committed robbery.

(c) A meets Z and Zs child on the high road. A takes the child and threatens to fling it down a precipice unless Z delivers his purse. Z, in consequence, delivers his purse. Here A has extorted the purse from Z by causing Z to be in fear of instant hurt to the child who is there present. A has therefore committed robbery on Z.

(d) A obtains property from Z by saying: 'Your child is in the hands of my gang, and will be put to death unless you send us ten thousand rupees '. This is extortion, and punishable as such; but it is not robbery, unless Z is put in fear of the instant death of his child.
data.name: penal_code_penalcodes.json_409

data.label: Theft
data.excerpt:
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, is said to commit theft.

Explanation 1

A thing so long as it is attached to the earth, not being movable property, is not the subject of theft; but it becomes capable of being the subject of theft as soon as it is severed from the earth.

Explanation 2

A moving effected by the same act which effects the severance may be a theft.

Explanation 3

A person is said to cause a thing to move by removing an obstacle which prevented it from moving, or by separating it from any other thing, as well as by actually moving it.

Explanation 4

A person who by any means causes an animal to move is said to move that animal and to move everything which in consequence of the motion so caused, is moved by that animal.

Explanation 5

The consent mentioned in the definition may be expressed or implied, and may be given either by the person in possession, or by any person having for that purpose authority either express or implied.

Illustrations

(a) A cuts down a tree on Z's ground, with the intention of dishonestly taking the tree out of Z's possession without Z's consent. Here, as soon as A has severed the tree, in order to such taking, he has committed theft.

(b) A puts a bait for dogs in his pocket, and thus induces Z's dog to follow it. Here, if As intention be dishonestly to take the dog out of Z's possession without Z's consent, A has committed theft as soon as Z's dog has begun to follow A.

(c) A meets a bullock carrying a box of treasure. He drives the bullock in a certain direction, in order that he may dishonestly take the treasure. As soon as the bullock begins to move A has committed theft of the treasure.

(d) A, being Z's servant, and entrusted by Z with the care of Z's plate, dishonestly runs away with the plate without Z's consent. A has committed theft.

(e) Z, going on a journey, entrusts his plate to A, the keeper of a warehouse, till Z shall return. A carries the plate to a goldsmith and sells it. Here the plate was not in Z's possession. It could not therefore be taken out of Z's possession, and A has not committed theft, though he may have committed criminal breach of trust.

(f) A finds a ring belonging to Z on a table in the house which Z occupies. Here the ring is in Z's possession, and if A dishonestly removes it, A commits theft.

(g) A finds a ring lying on the high road, not in the possession of any person. A, by taking it, commits no theft, though he may commit criminal misappropriation of property,

(h) A sees a ring belonging to Z lying on a table in Z's house. Not venturing to misappropriate the ring immediately for fear of search and detection, A hides the ring in a place where it is highly improbable that it will ever be found by Z, with the intention of taking the ring from the hiding place and selling it when the loss is forgotten- Here A. at the time of first moving the ring, commits theft.

(i) A delivers his watch to Z, a jeweler, to be regulated, Z carries it to his shop. A, not owing to the jeweler any debt for which the jeweler might lawfully detain the watch as security, enters the shop openly, takes his watch by force out of Z's hand, and carries it away. Here A, though he may have committed criminal trespass and assault, has not committed theft, inasmuch as what he did was not done dishonestly.

(j) If A owes money to Z for repairing the watch, and if Z retains the watch lawfully as a security for the debt, and A takes the watch out of Zs possession with the intention of depriving Z of the property as a security for his debt, he commits theft, inasmuch as he takes it dishonestly.

(k) Again, if A having pawned his watch to Z, takes it out of Z's possession without Z's consent, not having paid what he borrowed on the watch, he commits theft, though the watch is his own property, inasmuch as he takes it dishonestly.

(l) A takes an article belonging to Z out of Z's possession, without Z's consent, with the intention of keeping it until he obtains money from Z as a reward for its restoration. Here A takes dishonestly; A has therefore committed theft.

(m) A, being on friendly terms with Z, goes in to Z's library in Z's absence, and takes away a book without Z's express consent, for the purpose merely of reading it, and with the intention of returning it. Here, it is probable that A may have conceived that he had Z's implied consent to use Zs book. If this was A's impression, A has not committed theft.

(n) A asks charity from Z's wife. She gives A money, food, and clothes, which A knows to belong to Z, her husband. Here, it is probable that A may conceive that Z's wife is authorized to give away alms. If this was A's impression, A has not committed theft.

(o) A is the paramour of Z's wife. She gives A valuable property which A knows to belong to her husband Z and to be such property as she has not authority from Z to give. If A takes the property dishonestly, he commits theft.

(p) A in good faith, believing property belonging to Z to be A's own property, takes that property out of B's possession. Here, as A does not take dishonestly, he does not commit theft.
data.name: penal_code_penalcodes.json_396

data.label: Dishonest misappropriation of property
data.excerpt:
Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both

Illustration

(a) A takes property belonging to Z out of Z*'s possession, in good faith believing, at the time when he takes it, that the properly belongs to himself. A is not guilty of theft; but if A, after discovering his mistake, dishonestly appropriates the property to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

(b) A, being on friendly terms with Z, goes into Z's library in Z's absence, and takes away a book without Z's express consent. Here, if A was under the impression that he had Z's implied consent to take the book for the purpose of reading it. A has not committed theft. But if A afterwards sells the book for his own benefit, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

(c) A and B being joint owners of a horse, A takes the horse out of B's possession, intending to use it. Here as A has a right to use the horse, he does not dishonestly misappropriate it. But if A sells the horse and appropriates the whole proceeds to his own use, he is guilty of an offence under this section.

Explanation I

A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is misappropriation within the meaning of this section.

Illustration

A finds a promissory note belonging to Z, payable to bearer. A, knowing that the note belongs to Z, pledges it with a banker as security for a loan, intending at a future time to restore it to Z. A has committed an offence under this section.

Explanation 2

(i) A person who finds property not in the possession of any other person and takes such property for the purpose of protecting it for, or of restoring it to, the owner, does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly, and is not guilty of an offence; but he is guilty of the offence above defined if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner, or before he has used reasonable means to discover and give notice to the owner, and has kept the properly a reasonable time to enable the owner to claim it.
(ii) What are reasonable means, or what is a reasonable time in such a case, is a question of fact.
(iii) It is not necessary that the finder should know who is the owner of the property, or that any particular person is the owner of it; it is sufficient if, at the time of appropriating it, he does not believe it to be his own property, or in good faith believes that the real owner cannot be found.

Illustration

(a) A finds a rupee on the high road, not knowing to whom the rupee belongs. A picks up the rupee. Here A has not committed the offence defined in this section.
(b) A finds a letter on the road containing a bank note. From the direction and contents of the letter he learns to whom the note belongs. He appropriates the note. He is guilty of an offence under this section.
(c) A finds a cheque payable to bearer. He can form no conjecture as to the person who has lost the cheque. But the name of the person who has drawn the cheque appears. A knows that this person can direct him to the person in whose favour the cheque was drawn. A appropriates the cheque without attempting to discover the owner. He is guilty of an offence under this section.
(d) A sees Z drop no purse with money in it. A picks up the purse with the intention of restoring it to Z, but afterwards appropriates it to his own use. A has committed an offence under this section.
(e) A finds a purse with money not knowing to whom it belongs; he afterwards discovers that it belongs to Z, and appropriates it to his own use. A is guilty of an offence under this section.
(f) A finds a valuable ring, not knowing to whom it belongs. A sells it immediately, without attempting to discover the owner. A is guilty of an offence under this section.
data.name: penal_code_penalcodes.json_416

data.label: Criminal breach of trust
data.excerpt:
Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or willfully suffers any other person so to do, commits 'criminal breach of trust'.

Illustrations

(a) A, being executor to the will of a deceased person, dishonestly disobeys the law, which directs him to divide the effects according to the will, and appropriates them to his own use. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(b) A is a warehouse-keeper. Z, going on a journey. entrusts his furniture to A, under a contract that it shall be returned on payment of a stipulated sum for warehouse room. A dishonestly sells the goods. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(c) A, residing in Colombo, is agent for Z, residing in England, There is an express or implied contract between A and Z that all sums remitted by Z to A shall be Invested by A according to Z's direction, Z remits ten thousand rupees to A, with directions to A to invest the same on mortgage of coffee estates, A dishonestly disobeys the directions, and employs the money in his own business. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(d) But if A, in the last illustration, not dishonestly but in good faith, believing that it will be more for Z's advantage to hold shares in a company, disobeys Z's directions and buys shares in a company in Z's name instead of investing the money on mortgage, here, though Z should suffer loss, and should be entitled to bring a civil action against A on account of that loss, yet A not having acted dishonestly has not committed criminal breach of trust.

(e) A, a revenue officer, is entrusted with public money, and is either directed by law or bound by a contract, express or implied, with the Government, to pay into a certain Kachcheri all the public money which he holds. A dishonestly appropriates the money. A has committed criminal breach of trust.

(f) A, a carrier, is entrusted by Z with properly to be carried by land or by water. A dishonestly misappropriates the property. A has committed criminal breach of trust.
data.name: penal_code_penalcodes.json_418

data.label: Extortion
data.excerpt:
Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person or to any other and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits ' extortion '.

Illustrations

(a) A threatens to publish a defamatory libel concerning Z, unless Z gives him money. He thus induces Z to give him money. A has committed extortion.

(b) A threatens Z that he will keep Z's child in wrongful confinement, unless Z will sign and deliver to A promissory note binding Z to pay certain moneys to A, Z signs and delivers the note. A has committed extortion.

(c) A, by putting Z in fear of grievous hurt, dishonestly induces Z to sign or affix his seal to a blank paper and deliver it to A. Z signs and delivers the paper to A. Here, as the paper so signed may be converted into a valuable security, A has committed extortion.

(4) If refusal, the OIC may apply to the Magistrate for an order.

data.label: Thilakaratne v. Attorney-General
data.excerpt: [{'point_number': 1, 'summary': 'Where an accused is being tried in absentia under section 241(1) of the Criminal Procedure Code, it is undesirable for the court to assign a counsel to defend him without his consent. Although intended for his benefit, such a step would trigger section 241(4), thereby depriving the accused of the valuable right to re-open the proceedings under section 241(3) if he later proves his absence was bona fide.'}, {'point_number': 2, 'summary': 'Section 195(g) of the Criminal Procedure Code, which provides for assigning counsel, contemplates a situation where the accused is physically present in court and requests counsel upon being questioned.'}, {'point_number': 3, 'summary': 'Section 241(2) does not make it obligatory for the court to assign counsel to an absent accused. This section applies where an absent accused has retained counsel or where a counsel continues to appear for an accused who absconds during the trial.'}] The 1st, 2nd, and 3rd accused were charged in the High Court of Anuradhapura on two counts: (1) Robbery of cash Rs. 18,552/40 and a bicycle, causing hurt to Ukkubanda Semasinghe, punishable under sections 380 and 382 read with section 32 of the Penal Code; and (2) Robbery of a bicycle from Punchi Bandage Ratnayake, punishable under section 380 read with section 32. The accused appeared on 22.9.1986 represented by counsel. On the trial date 17.1.86, the 1st accused was absent without excuse. A warrant was issued for 20.1.87. On 20.1.87, the 1st accused was initially present in the court premises but absconded before the case was called. His surety testified that the accused had come to court but left. The trial Judge, satisfied that the 1st accused was absconding, proceeded with the trial in absentia. The 2nd and 3rd accused pleaded guilty and were sentenced. The trial proceeded against the 1st accused. Evidence was led showing the accused had robbed a school principal and teacher of payroll money, armed with a pistol and knife. The 1st accused was found guilty and sentenced to 10 years rigorous imprisonment and fines. The 1st accused was arrested and produced in court on 7.12.87. On 13.1.88, his counsel submitted he had been unwell but tendered no medical certificate. The High Court Judge refused bail and ordered the sentence to be carried out. No application was made under section 241(3) of the Criminal Procedure Code to show bona fide absence and reopen the case. An appeal was filed arguing insufficient evidence of absconding and failure to assign counsel. The Court rejected the ground that there was insufficient evidence of absconding. The accused was present on the morning of the trial but left without excuse. It was only a year later that a claim of illness was made, unsupported by any medical certificate. The conclusion that the 1st accused was absconding was inescapable. The Court addressed the argument that the failure to assign counsel vitiated the conviction. Section 195(g) applies only when an accused is present. While Section 241(2) ensures an absent accused's right to be defended is not prejudiced, it does not mandate the court to assign counsel. Crucially, Section 241(4) prevents an accused defended by an attorney from reopening proceedings under Section 241(3). Therefore, assigning counsel to an absent accused without consent is detrimental as it deprives them of the statutory right to reopen the case if they can prove bona fide absence. The trial Judge's conduct in not assigning counsel was correct. The Court considered the disparity in sentences between the 1st accused (10 years) and the others (3 years). The disparity was justified because the 1st accused was armed with a pistol, fired a shot, caused extensive injuries with a knife, and had previous convictions. The appeal was dismissed, and the conviction and sentences were affirmed.
data.name: landmark_case_landmark5.json_0

data.label: K. G. Somapala v. The Attorney-General
data.excerpt: [{'point_number': 1, 'summary': 'In a criminal prosecution, evidence of the bad character of the accused, which is not necessary or relevant to prove the alleged motive for the offence, is inadmissible.'}, {'point_number': 2, 'summary': "Where the prosecution led evidence that the accused's work was stopped because he had attempted to stab an Engineer, purportedly to prove motive for assaulting a complainant suspected of instigating the stoppage, such evidence was held to be inadmissible as it related to bad character and was not necessary to prove the motive."}] The Accused-Appellant was charged for voluntarily causing hurt to one B. Robert Silva, a driver in the Ceylon Transport Board with hands, punishable under Section 314 of the Ceylon Penal Code. Accused was a mechanic employed in the Ceylon Transport Board. He was found guilty of the charge and was sentenced to 2 months' rigorous imprisonment and to pay a fine of Rs. 50/-. In default of payment of the fine, he was sentenced to one month's rigorous imprisonment. At the trial, the prosecution led evidence of motive for this offence. Robert Silva stated that the accused was under the impression that he had instigated the Engineer to stop the accused's work. He further stated that accused's work was stopped as a result of his attempt to stab the Engineer of the Ceylon Transport Board. Clearly the evidence that accused's work was stopped because he attempted to stab the Engineer of the Ceylon Transport Board was not necessary or relevant for the purpose of proving the motive that the accused had against the complainant Robert Silva. The alleged motive that Accused had against the Complainant was that he suspected that the Complainant was instrumental in having him interdicted from work over another incident. Learned Crown Counsel conceded that this evidence was inadmissible to prove the alleged motive. He argued, however, that no prejudice has been caused to the accused as the learned Magistrate has not referred to this aspect of the evidence in his reasons for the conviction. It is quite clear in this case that the Magistrate has taken a very serious view of the offence of this accused in view of the sentence passed in this case. There is no doubt that he was strongly influenced by the evidence that the accused had previously attempted to stab the C. T. B. Engineer. He commenced his reasons by stating, 'The sooner the Ceylon Transport Board gets rid of undesirable people like the accused the better it is for this establishment'. Even if it can be said in this case which I am not prepared to do, that the learned Magistrate was in fact not prejudiced against the accused by this inadmissible evidence of accused's previous bad character, this evidence is very likely to prejudice any judge deciding this case. In V. Coomaraswamy v. M. Meera Saibo and 5 others it was held 'It is not suggested that the learned Magistrate was influenced by these statements but the accused may very well have gained the impression that he was so influenced. It is necessary not only that administration of justice should be pure but it should be seen to be pure and considered to be pure'. In this case I have carefully considered whether this case should be sent back for a retrial as the conviction of the accused cannot possibly stand. Considering the trivial nature of the charge and the nature of the evidence in support of the prosecution case, I do not think that the accused should be put to the trouble and expense of standing another trial in this case. I accordingly allow the appeal and set aside the conviction and sentence of the accused and acquit him.
data.name: landmark_case_landmark3.json_0

data.label: The Queen v. M. Sathasivam
data.excerpt: [{'point_number': 1, 'summary': "A letter written by a deceased person to the police expressing fear or suspicion of the accused, in anticipation of the accused's return from abroad, is not admissible under Section 32(1) of the Evidence Ordinance if it amounts to mere general expressions not directly related to the circumstances of the transaction resulting in death."}, {'point_number': 2, 'summary': 'Such a letter is not admissible to prove motive under Section 8(1) of the Evidence Ordinance unless there is independent evidence that the allegations in the letter had come to the knowledge of the accused and induced resentment in his mind.'}, {'point_number': 3, 'summary': 'The letter is not admissible to prove conduct of the deceased under Section 8(2) read with Section 11 unless there is independent evidence to support the suggestion that the deceased was apprehensive of danger to her safety.'}, {'point_number': 4, 'summary': 'Evidence of trifling weight which is gravely prejudicial to the accused, even if technically admissible, ought to be excluded by the Judge if its potential prejudice is out of proportion to its true evidential value.'}] The prisoner is charged with murdering his wife on the morning of 9th October, 1951. The prisoner had been in England while the deceased remained in Ceylon. Divorce proceedings for malicious desertion had been instituted by the deceased. The Crown sought to introduce a letter (P24) written by the deceased on 17th September 1951 to the Superintendent of Police, Colombo, anticipating the prisoner's return and expressing fear that he might use violence and requesting police protection. The defence objected to the reception of the letter P24. The Solicitor-General argued it was admissible under Section 32(1) of the Evidence Ordinance. The Court held that the statements in the letter amounted to mere general expressions indicating fear or suspicion and were not directly related to the occasion of her death. Citing Narayana Swami v. Emperor, the Court ruled that the circumstances must have some proximate relation to the actual occurrence. Thus, the letter was not admissible under Section 32(1). The Crown argued the letter was relevant under Section 8(1) to establish motive. The theory was that the prisoner's knowledge of the letter caused resentment. The Court held that for this to be admissible, the Crown must prove the prisoner actually knew of the letter and resented it. Without independent evidence of such knowledge and resentment, the letter could not be admitted to prove motive. The Court distinguished R. v. Buckley, where prior threats were admitted because there was a direct link to the motive. The Crown alternatively argued admissibility under Section 8(2) as evidence of the deceased's conduct (seeking police protection due to fear). The Court found this relevance remote. The suggestion that her cordiality towards the accused was feigned due to fear required independent evidence of her apprehension. Until such evidence was forthcoming, Section 8(2) did not apply. The Court distinguished the Indian case Golak Behari Takal v. The Emperor, noting that in that case, the complaint related to the specific crime (conspiracy) under investigation. The Court emphasized the principle from Noor Mohamed v. The King and Harris v. D.P.P. that a Judge should exclude evidence that has only trifling weight but is gravely prejudicial to the accused. The evidential value of P24 standing alone was slender, while its prejudicial effect on the jury would be substantial. Justice required that unless the truth of the document's contents could be proved by independent evidence for a relevant purpose, it should be excluded to ensure a fair trial. The objection to the reception of the letter P24 and related oral evidence was upheld. The document was ruled out for the present, subject to any fresh application by the Crown if independent evidence establishing its relevance became available later.
data.name: landmark_case_landmark2.json_0
